clinicalStem: A 22-year-old male university student is brought to the emergency department by his parents after three weeks of hearing voices telling him he is being monitored by the government, refusing to leave his room, and expressing the belief that his food is being poisoned. He has no significant past medical history and takes no medications. Physical examination and basic metabolic panel are unremarkable. He is started on an oral antipsychotic. Six weeks later he returns reporting significant stiffness, restlessness, and an inability to sit still. On examination he has cogwheel rigidity and is pacing continuously. Which antipsychotic is MOST likely responsible for this adverse effect profile?
options: Clozapine
Quetiapine
Haloperidol
Olanzapine
Aripiprazole
explanation: Haloperidol is a high-potency first-generation (typical) antipsychotic with strong D2 receptor blockade, producing a high rate of extrapyramidal side effects (EPS) including parkinsonian rigidity (cogwheel rigidity), akathisia (inability to sit still, pacing), and acute dystonias. The combination of cogwheel rigidity and akathisia strongly points to a high-potency D2 blocker. Second-generation antipsychotics have lower EPS risk due to lower D2 affinity and concurrent 5-HT2A blockade.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Clozapine has very low EPS risk due to its low D2 affinity; its main concerns are agranulocytosis and metabolic syndrome, not cogwheel rigidity.
Quetiapine has one of the lowest EPS profiles among antipsychotics because of its transient, low-affinity D2 binding; it rarely causes akathisia or parkinsonism.
Haloperidol is correct — it is a high-potency typical antipsychotic with the highest EPS burden in the class.
Olanzapine has moderate EPS risk but is primarily associated with metabolic side effects (weight gain, dyslipidaemia); it rarely causes pronounced cogwheel rigidity and akathisia together.
Aripiprazole is a partial D2 agonist with low EPS risk; it may cause mild akathisia but not cogwheel rigidity at this level.
clinicalPearl: High-potency first-generation antipsychotics (haloperidol, fluphenazine) have the highest extrapyramidal side-effect burden; cogwheel rigidity plus akathisia together are a classic EPS cluster.
keyClue: Cogwheel rigidity combined with akathisia (pacing, restlessness) after starting an antipsychotic points to high D2 blockade.
commonExamTrap: Students may choose olanzapine because it is commonly prescribed, but its EPS profile is much milder than haloperidol.
missedConcept: Failure to distinguish first-generation (typical) from second-generation (atypical) antipsychotics and their differing EPS risk profiles.

clinicalStem: A 34-year-old woman with treatment-resistant schizophrenia is being initiated on clozapine after failing two adequate trials of other antipsychotics. Her baseline full blood count shows a white cell count of 6.8 × 10⁹/L and an absolute neutrophil count (ANC) of 3.2 × 10⁹/L. The treating psychiatrist explains that mandatory haematological monitoring is required. Which monitoring schedule is MOST appropriate during the FIRST 18 weeks of clozapine therapy?
options: Monthly full blood count for the first 18 weeks
Weekly full blood count for the first 18 weeks
Full blood count at baseline and at 6 weeks only
Fortnightly full blood count for the first 18 weeks
Full blood count only if the patient becomes febrile
explanation: Clozapine causes agranulocytosis in approximately 1–2% of patients, with the highest risk during the first 18 weeks of therapy. Regulatory guidelines mandate weekly full blood counts (including absolute neutrophil count) during the first 18 weeks to detect early neutropenia and prevent potentially fatal agranulocytosis. After 18 weeks of normal counts, monitoring can be reduced to fortnightly, and after one year to monthly.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Monthly monitoring is the schedule after one full year of therapy with consistently normal counts; it is insufficient during the highest-risk period.
Weekly FBC for the first 18 weeks is the correct, mandated schedule — clozapine cannot be dispensed without this monitoring in most countries.
A single check at 6 weeks is dangerously inadequate; agranulocytosis can develop rapidly and without warning at any point in the first 18 weeks.
Fortnightly monitoring is the schedule used after the first 18 weeks through to the end of the first year — it is not appropriate for the initial high-risk period.
Waiting for fever before checking the FBC defeats the purpose of surveillance; neutropenic patients may not mount a fever and can deteriorate rapidly.
clinicalPearl: The clozapine monitoring rule: weekly FBC for 18 weeks → fortnightly for weeks 18–52 → monthly thereafter. Dispensing is tied to blood results in most registries.
keyClue: 'First 18 weeks' is the highest-risk window for clozapine-induced agranulocytosis, mandating weekly haematological surveillance.
commonExamTrap: Confusing the fortnightly (weeks 18–52) schedule with the initial weekly requirement.
missedConcept: Clozapine has a mandatory monitoring programme; the frequency is tied directly to time since initiation.

clinicalStem: A 27-year-old man with chronic schizophrenia is admitted via the emergency department. His care home staff report that he was started on a higher dose of haloperidol four days ago. On examination: temperature 39.8°C, heart rate 128 bpm, blood pressure 158/100 mmHg, respiratory rate 24/min. He is confused and has severe lead-pipe rigidity throughout. Investigations reveal CK 18,400 U/L, creatinine 198 µmol/L, and white cell count 16.2 × 10⁹/L. What is the MOST important immediate management step?
options: Administer oral lorazepam and monitor in the ward
Increase the haloperidol dose to control agitation
Discontinue haloperidol immediately and commence IV fluids with supportive care
Administer oral bromocriptine and discharge with outpatient follow-up
Start IV antibiotics for presumed sepsis and await cultures
explanation: Neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS) is a life-threatening emergency characterised by hyperthermia, severe muscle rigidity, autonomic instability, and altered consciousness following antipsychotic use. The immediate priority is to stop the offending antipsychotic, provide aggressive IV hydration to prevent acute kidney injury from rhabdomyolysis (CK 18,400), and give supportive care. Bromocriptine (dopamine agonist) and dantrolene may be adjuncts but the first step is always discontinuation and IV fluids.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Lorazepam alone and ward monitoring is insufficient; NMS with this degree of rigidity, hyperthermia, and rhabdomyolysis requires immediate antipsychotic cessation and intensive supportive care.
Increasing the haloperidol dose would worsen NMS and could be fatal — the antipsychotic must be stopped, not escalated.
Stopping haloperidol and starting IV fluids is the correct, most immediate intervention in NMS with rhabdomyolysis.
Bromocriptine may be used as an adjunct but oral administration and outpatient discharge are completely inappropriate in a patient with temperature 39.8°C, AKI, and CK >18,000.

Although sepsis should be considered, the clinical triad of hyperthermia + lead-pipe rigidity + markedly elevated CK after antipsychotic dose increase is diagnostic of NMS; antibiotics alone without stopping the causative drug is dangerous.
clinicalPearl: NMS = hyperthermia + lead-pipe rigidity + autonomic instability + elevated CK after antipsychotic. STOP the drug immediately and start IV fluids — rhabdomyolysis-induced AKI is the primary killer.
keyClue: Recent haloperidol dose increase + hyperthermia + lead-pipe rigidity + CK 18,400 = NMS until proven otherwise.
commonExamTrap: Mistaking NMS for sepsis or serotonin syndrome; serotonin syndrome causes clonus and hyperreflexia, not lead-pipe rigidity.
missedConcept: In NMS the antipsychotic must be stopped immediately; continuing or increasing it worsens outcome.
redFlagReason: Neuroleptic malignant syndrome with rhabdomyolysis and acute kidney injury is immediately life-threatening; failure to stop the antipsychotic and give aggressive IV fluids can result in death.

clinicalStem: A 58-year-old woman with a 20-year history of recurrent depressive disorder is admitted following a significant decline in her mental state. She has not eaten or drunk anything for four days, has lost 8 kg over the past month, and is mute. She has a fixed delusional belief that her intestines have rotted away (nihilistic delusion). Two adequate antidepressant trials at therapeutic doses over the past four months have produced no improvement. Her physical observations are stable. Which treatment is MOST appropriate at this stage?
options: Add lithium augmentation to her current antidepressant
Switch to a monoamine oxidase inhibitor
Commence electroconvulsive therapy (ECT)
Initiate clozapine for treatment-resistant depression
Increase psychotherapy sessions to daily frequency
explanation: ECT is the treatment of choice when depression is severe, life-threatening (not eating/drinking for four days creates medical risk), or accompanied by psychotic features such as nihilistic delusions. ECT is also indicated when two adequate antidepressant trials have failed. This patient meets multiple absolute indications: life-threatening illness, psychotic depression, and treatment resistance. ECT has a faster response rate than any pharmacological intervention in this context.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Lithium augmentation is a valid strategy for treatment-resistant depression, but this patient is medically compromised by dehydration and has psychotic features — the urgency and severity mandate ECT rather than waiting another 4–6 weeks for augmentation to work.
MAOIs are rarely used as first-line and require a washout period from other antidepressants; they are not appropriate as the next step given the acute severity.
ECT is the most appropriate intervention given life-threatening severity, psychotic depression, and failure of two adequate antidepressant trials.
Clozapine is used for treatment-resistant schizophrenia, not as a primary treatment for psychotic depression.
Psychotherapy alone is completely insufficient in a mute, medically compromised patient with nihilistic delusions and severe weight loss.
clinicalPearl: ECT indications include: severe/life-threatening depression (not eating/drinking), psychotic depression, two failed antidepressant trials, catatonia, and patient preference. Nihilistic delusions are a classic psychotic depression feature.
keyClue: Mute, not eating for 4 days, nihilistic delusions, two antidepressant failures = multiple ECT indications converging.
commonExamTrap: Students may choose lithium augmentation as a safe pharmacological next step, missing the urgency of medical compromise and psychotic features that mandate ECT.
missedConcept: Psychotic depression with life-threatening refusal to eat is an absolute indication for ECT, not simply a reason to try another drug.

clinicalStem: A 70-year-old man with severe treatment-resistant depression is being considered for ECT. He has a background of ischaemic heart disease, hypertension, and a cerebral aneurysm that was clipped surgically 12 years ago. Current medications include aspirin, amlodipine, and ramipril. His most recent ECG shows normal sinus rhythm. Pre-ECT investigations are being planned. Which condition, if present and UNCONTROLLED, would represent the GREATEST risk for proceeding with ECT?
options: Mild osteoporosis on DEXA scan
Elevated intracranial pressure from an undiagnosed space-occupying lesion
Well-controlled hypertension with systolic BP 130 mmHg
History of surgically clipped cerebral aneurysm 12 years ago
A previous allergic reaction to penicillin
explanation: Elevated intracranial pressure (ICP) is the most significant contraindication to ECT because the seizure-induced surge in cerebral blood flow and ICP can cause cerebral herniation. An undiagnosed space-occupying lesion with raised ICP is therefore a near-absolute contraindication requiring exclusion before ECT is given. All other listed conditions either require optimisation (hypertension) or represent much lower risks — there are no absolute contraindications to ECT, but raised ICP is the closest.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Osteoporosis increases the theoretical risk of vertebral fracture during ECT, but modern muscle relaxants (succinylcholine) virtually eliminate this risk; it is a relative concern, not the greatest risk.
Uncontrolled raised intracranial pressure from a space-occupying lesion is the greatest risk — the seizure-induced ICP surge can cause fatal herniation.
Well-controlled hypertension (BP 130 mmHg) is not a contraindication; blood pressure is optimised pre-ECT and monitored throughout. Poorly controlled hypertension would be a relative concern.
A successfully clipped aneurysm from 12 years ago is not a contraindication to ECT; the aneurysm has been surgically eliminated. It would be relevant if the aneurysm were unclipped.
Penicillin allergy is irrelevant to ECT; the anaesthetic agents used (propofol, succinylcholine) are unrelated to penicillin.
clinicalPearl: Raised intracranial pressure is the closest to an absolute contraindication for ECT. Always exclude a space-occupying lesion with brain imaging before initiating ECT in atypical presentations.
keyClue: Uncontrolled intracranial hypertension is dangerous during ECT because the induced seizure causes a further ICP surge that risks herniation.
commonExamTrap: Students often choose the clipped aneurysm as the major risk, forgetting that a successfully treated aneurysm poses minimal additional risk compared to uncontrolled ICP.
missedConcept: ECT has no absolute contraindications in most guidelines, but raised ICP represents the highest risk scenario and must be excluded.

clinicalStem: A 45-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder has been stable on lithium 800 mg daily for three years, with lithium levels consistently between 0.7 and 0.9 mmol/L. She presents to her GP with a one-week history of nausea, coarse tremor, and confusion. Her husband reports she started taking ibuprofen 400 mg three times daily one week ago for knee pain. Her most recent lithium level, taken today, is 1.9 mmol/L. Which mechanism BEST explains the rise in her lithium level?
options: Ibuprofen inhibits hepatic cytochrome P450 enzymes, reducing lithium metabolism
Ibuprofen reduces renal prostaglandin synthesis, decreasing renal lithium clearance
Ibuprofen displaces lithium from plasma protein binding sites
Ibuprofen inhibits the renal sodium-lithium countertransporter directly
Ibuprofen increases gastrointestinal absorption of lithium
explanation: NSAIDs (including ibuprofen) inhibit prostaglandin synthesis in the kidney, which reduces renal blood flow and decreases glomerular filtration rate. Since lithium is filtered and reabsorbed in the proximal tubule alongside sodium, any reduction in renal perfusion or sodium depletion causes compensatory tubular sodium (and lithium) reabsorption, resulting in lithium retention and toxicity. Lithium is not hepatically metabolised and is not protein-bound, making the other options mechanistically incorrect.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Lithium is not metabolised by hepatic cytochrome P450 enzymes; it is excreted unchanged by the kidneys, so this mechanism does not apply.
NSAIDs reduce prostaglandin-dependent renal blood flow, leading to decreased lithium clearance and toxicity — this is the correct mechanism.
Lithium has negligible plasma protein binding (virtually 0%), so displacement from binding sites cannot raise free lithium levels.
There is no evidence that NSAIDs directly inhibit the sodium-lithium countertransporter; their nephrotoxic effect is via prostaglandin inhibition and reduced renal perfusion.
Ibuprofen does not increase gastrointestinal lithium absorption; the interaction is entirely renal.
clinicalPearl: NSAIDs are a classic cause of lithium toxicity by reducing renal prostaglandins, lowering GFR, and causing compensatory lithium retention. Advise all patients on lithium to avoid NSAIDs and use paracetamol instead.
keyClue: Recent NSAID initiation + confusion + coarse tremor + lithium level 1.9 mmol/L = NSAID-induced lithium toxicity via reduced renal clearance.
commonExamTrap: Assuming a drug interaction must involve hepatic metabolism — lithium has no hepatic metabolism, so the interaction is purely renal.
missedConcept: Lithium excretion tracks sodium; any state that causes sodium/water retention (NSAIDs, thiazides, dehydration) will also retain lithium.
redFlagReason: Lithium toxicity at 1.9 mmol/L with neurological symptoms (confusion, coarse tremor) is a medical emergency requiring urgent management to prevent seizures, cardiac arrhythmias, and permanent neurological damage.

clinicalStem: A 38-year-old man with a confirmed diagnosis of bipolar I disorder is currently on lithium 1000 mg/day (level 0.75 mmol/L) for mood stabilisation. He develops a moderate depressive episode with low mood, anhedonia, hypersomnia, and psychomotor retardation for the past six weeks. He has no psychotic features. His GP is considering adding an antidepressant. Which statement BEST describes the most appropriate pharmacological approach?
options: Add a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI) as first-line augmentation without further consideration
Optimise the lithium level to 0.8–1.0 mmol/L before adding any antidepressant
Quetiapine monotherapy (or added to lithium) is the preferred evidence-based option for bipolar depression
Commence a tricyclic antidepressant as it has the strongest evidence base in bipolar depression
Antidepressants are absolutely contraindicated in bipolar disorder and must never be used
explanation: Quetiapine has the strongest evidence base for bipolar depression and is approved for this indication. It can be used as monotherapy or added to a mood stabiliser. SSRIs can precipitate hypomania or mania and should not be started without mood stabiliser cover; they are not first-line. Tricyclic antidepressants have a higher switch risk to mania than SSRIs and are not preferred. Antidepressants are not absolutely contraindicated but require careful consideration and ideally mood stabiliser cover.
wrongAnswerExplanations: SSRIs should not be added as first-line without careful consideration of switch risk; if used at all in bipolar depression they require concurrent mood stabiliser therapy and are not the preferred first step.
Optimising lithium to 0.8–1.0 mmol/L is a reasonable supporting measure, but quetiapine added to mood stabiliser therapy has direct evidence for treating bipolar depression and is a better answer.
Quetiapine is the correct answer — it has RCT evidence, regulatory approval, and treats both depressive and manic phases of bipolar disorder.
TCAs have the highest risk of precipitating a manic switch among antidepressants and are not recommended in bipolar depression.
Antidepressants are not absolutely contraindicated, but they must be used cautiously and with mood stabiliser cover; this all-or-nothing statement is incorrect.
clinicalPearl: In bipolar depression, quetiapine (and lamotrigine) have the best evidence. SSRIs used without mood stabiliser cover risk precipitating mania or rapid cycling. Avoid TCAs in bipolar patients.
keyClue: Bipolar I patient on lithium + new depressive episode = the answer tests knowledge of evidence-based treatment for bipolar depression, not unipolar depression.
commonExamTrap: Treating bipolar depression identically to unipolar depression by jumping straight to an SSRI — this risks precipitating a manic episode.
missedConcept: Bipolar depression and unipolar depression require different pharmacological strategies; antidepressant monotherapy in bipolar disorder can destabilise the patient.

clinicalStem: A 52-year-old divorced man attends his GP for a review of his antidepressant prescription. He was started on sertraline six weeks ago for a moderate depressive episode. He reports that his mood is 'a bit better' but mentions he has been thinking about whether his family would be better off without him. He lives alone, recently lost his job, and has a history of alcohol dependence (currently in remission). He has no previous psychiatric admissions. Which feature in this presentation represents the HIGHEST risk factor for completed suicide?
options: Being male and aged over 50
Recent partial antidepressant response at six weeks
Social isolation, unemployment, and previous alcohol dependence
Passive suicidal ideation ('better off without him') with no specific plan
Lack of previous psychiatric admissions
explanation: Multiple co-existing psychosocial risk factors dramatically increase suicide risk. This patient has social isolation (living alone), unemployment (recent job loss), and a history of substance dependence — each independently elevates suicide risk, and their combination creates a significantly elevated risk profile. Being male and older does increase risk statistically, but the convergence of social determinants is the most modifiable and clinically actionable highest-risk cluster here.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Male sex and age over 50 do increase statistical risk for completed suicide, but they are non-modifiable demographic factors. The convergence of social stressors is the more clinically salient and actionable risk cluster.

Partial antidepressant response at 6 weeks is actually not a high-risk feature per se; paradoxically, early partial response (returning energy before mood lifts) can transiently increase risk, but this alone is not the highest-risk factor here.
Social isolation + unemployment + past alcohol dependence is the cluster of modifiable, co-existing risk factors that most significantly elevates his overall risk — this is the correct answer.
Passive suicidal ideation without a plan is concerning and must be assessed further, but the absence of a specific plan lowers immediate risk compared to patients with active plans and intent.
Lack of previous psychiatric admissions actually slightly lowers risk (previous attempts and admissions are strong predictors); this is not a high-risk feature.
clinicalPearl: Suicide risk is multiplicative: male sex + older age + social isolation + unemployment + substance misuse history = high-risk constellation. Always screen for plan, intent, means, and protective factors.
keyClue: The convergence of multiple independent risk factors (isolation, unemployment, alcohol dependence history) creates the highest-risk profile in this vignette.
commonExamTrap: Focusing only on demographics (male, >50) and missing the psychosocial risk cluster which is more clinically actionable.
missedConcept: Suicide risk assessment requires integrating static (demographic) and dynamic (psychosocial, clinical) risk factors; no single factor alone defines risk.
redFlagReason: Suicidal ideation in a high-risk patient (multiple co-existing risk factors) requires urgent risk assessment; underestimating this risk can lead to preventable death.

clinicalStem: A 28-year-old woman with a diagnosis of borderline personality disorder (BPD) presents to her psychiatrist reporting intense mood swings lasting hours, impulsive self-harm when upset, and brief episodes of hearing her name called when alone. She is currently not on any psychotropic medication. She asks about medication to help with her symptoms. Which statement BEST reflects the evidence-based pharmacological approach to BPD?
options: High-dose SSRIs are the definitive treatment for BPD and eliminate core symptoms
Antipsychotic medications (e.g., low-dose olanzapine) may target impulsivity, brief psychotic symptoms, and affective dysregulation
Lithium is the first-choice mood stabiliser for BPD and should be started immediately
Benzodiazepines are the safest long-term pharmacological option for emotional dysregulation in BPD
Pharmacotherapy is the primary treatment for BPD and should precede psychotherapy
explanation: In BPD, pharmacotherapy is adjunctive (not primary) — psychotherapy, particularly Dialectical Behaviour Therapy (DBT), is the first-line treatment. Low-dose antipsychotics (e.g., olanzapine, quetiapine) have evidence for targeting impulsivity, brief psychotic-like symptoms (transient stress-related paranoia, quasi-psychosis), and affective instability. SSRIs may help specific symptoms (anger, affective reactivity) but do not treat core BPD features. Benzodiazepines are risky due to addiction potential and paradoxical disinhibition in BPD.
wrongAnswerExplanations: SSRIs have limited and inconsistent evidence in BPD; they do not eliminate core symptoms and are not definitive treatment. High-dose SSRIs are not standard practice for BPD.
Low-dose antipsychotics targeting impulsivity, brief psychotic symptoms, and affective dysregulation — this is the best pharmacological option with supporting evidence in BPD guidelines.
Lithium has weak evidence in BPD; it is not first-choice and has significant monitoring requirements. Valproate may have slightly more evidence for impulsivity in BPD than lithium.
Benzodiazepines carry high risk of dependence in BPD patients and may cause disinhibition, potentially worsening impulsive and self-harm behaviours. They are not recommended long-term.
Psychotherapy (DBT) is the primary treatment for BPD; pharmacotherapy is adjunctive and symptom-targeted, not primary.
clinicalPearl: BPD: psychotherapy (DBT) is primary. Low-dose antipsychotics target the impulsivity/micro-psychotic/affective cluster. Avoid long-term benzodiazepines due to disinhibition and dependence risk.
keyClue: Brief psychotic experiences + impulsivity + affective dysregulation in BPD cluster together; antipsychotics address this triad better than SSRIs alone.
commonExamTrap: Choosing SSRIs as definitive treatment, or placing pharmacotherapy above psychotherapy for BPD — DBT is always first-line.
missedConcept: BPD pharmacotherapy is symptom-targeted and adjunctive; no medication treats the disorder itself, only specific symptom domains.

subtopic: Early identification and prevention of harm
clinicalStem: A 32-year-old woman is referred to a community mental health team by her GP. Over the past ten years she has had multiple episodes of self-harm, eight different antidepressant trials with poor or transient responses, four brief inpatient admissions, and describes intense but unstable relationships. She reports feeling chronically empty and has frequent angry outbursts. She was never given a formal personality disorder assessment. Which is the MOST appropriate validated screening instrument to identify a personality disorder in this case?
options: Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9)
Standardised Assessment of Personality — Abbreviated Scale (SAPAS)
Mini-Mental State Examination (MMSE)
Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale (EPDS)
Generalised Anxiety Disorder-7 (GAD-7)
explanation: The SAPAS (Standardised Assessment of Personality — Abbreviated Scale) is an 8-item validated screening tool specifically designed to identify personality disorder in clinical populations. It is the recommended first-step screening instrument in many national guidelines for identifying personality disorder in adults presenting to mental health services. PHQ-9 and GAD-7 screen for depression and anxiety respectively, and MMSE assesses cognitive function — none of these screen for personality pathology.
wrongAnswerExplanations: The PHQ-9 screens for depressive symptoms severity; it cannot identify or screen for personality disorder.
The SAPAS is the validated personality disorder screening tool appropriate for this patient — this is the correct answer.
The MMSE assesses cognitive function and dementia; it has no role in personality disorder screening.
The EPDS is used to screen for postnatal depression; it is irrelevant in this context.
The GAD-7 screens for generalised anxiety disorder severity; it does not assess personality pathology.
clinicalPearl: SAPAS is the standard validated first-step screening tool for personality disorder in secondary care. A pattern of failed antidepressants + self-harm + unstable relationships + chronic emptiness = always screen for personality disorder before continuing with symptom-focused treatment.
keyClue: Years of treatment-resistant depression, recurrent self-harm, unstable relationships, and chronic emptiness suggest underlying personality pathology requiring specific screening.
commonExamTrap: Continuing to treat presumed 'treatment-resistant depression' without screening for personality disorder, which changes the entire management approach.
missedConcept: Personality disorders are frequently missed in patients presenting as 'treatment-resistant depression'; SAPAS is the tool to screen for personality pathology.

subtopic: Prevention and management of ECT adverse effects
clinicalStem: A 65-year-old retired teacher with severe recurrent depressive disorder is completing a course of bilateral ECT and has responded well with significantly improved mood. However, she reports being unable to recall events from three months before her treatment and cannot remember conversations with her grandchildren during that period. She is distressed by these memory problems. Which statement BEST describes the nature and management of this side effect?
options: This represents permanent anterograde amnesia that will not resolve and requires neurological review
Retrograde autobiographical amnesia is a recognised ECT side effect; switching to right unilateral electrode placement and/or reducing frequency may minimise further cognitive effects
Memory loss after ECT always indicates an undiagnosed dementia triggered by anaesthesia
The cognitive side effects of ECT are only seen at suprathreshold stimulus intensities and can be eliminated by using threshold dosing
Bilateral ECT causes permanent hippocampal damage visible on MRI and should be discontinued immediately
explanation: Retrograde autobiographical memory loss for the period around and before ECT treatment is the most common significant cognitive side effect of ECT. It is generally reversible, though some patients retain patchy autobiographical gaps. Strategies to reduce cognitive burden include switching from bilateral to right unilateral electrode placement (equivalent efficacy with lower cognitive side effects at adequate intensity), reducing stimulus frequency (e.g., twice rather than three times weekly), and using ultrabrief pulse width. ECT does not cause dementia or permanent hippocampal damage visible on MRI.
wrongAnswerExplanations: The amnesia described is retrograde (not anterograde) and is typically not permanent; most autobiographical memories return over weeks to months, though some gaps may persist.
This is the correct answer: retrograde amnesia is recognised and strategies such as right unilateral placement and reduced frequency can mitigate ongoing cognitive effects.
ECT does not cause or trigger dementia; transient cognitive effects including confusion and memory impairment are common but are not indicative of an underlying neurodegenerative process.

Cognitive side effects occur across a range of stimulus intensities; while suprathreshold bilateral ECT carries higher cognitive burden, threshold dosing alone does not eliminate cognitive effects and may reduce therapeutic efficacy.

ECT does not cause permanent hippocampal damage on MRI in humans; this is a myth. Immediate discontinuation purely for cognitive side effects that are manageable with electrode/parameter adjustment would be premature.
clinicalPearl: ECT retrograde amnesia is usually reversible. To reduce cognitive side effects: switch to right unilateral electrode placement, use ultrabrief pulse width, or reduce treatment frequency. Never abandon ECT for manageable cognitive effects without trying parameter adjustments first.
keyClue: Retrograde amnesia (loss of pre-ECT memories) is the hallmark cognitive side effect of ECT; it is usually reversible and manageable.
commonExamTrap: Confusing retrograde amnesia (loss of past memories) with anterograde amnesia (inability to form new memories); both can occur with ECT but have different implications.
missedConcept: ECT parameters (electrode placement, pulse width, frequency) are the primary levers for reducing cognitive side effects while maintaining efficacy.

clinicalStem: A 30-year-old woman with a first episode of major depressive disorder has been on fluoxetine 20 mg daily for eight months and has been in full remission for the past five months. She feels well and wishes to stop her medication. She asks her GP how she should stop taking fluoxetine. Which advice is MOST appropriate?
options: Stop fluoxetine immediately as it has a long half-life and does not require tapering
Taper fluoxetine over a minimum of four weeks before stopping completely
Switch to paroxetine before stopping as it is safer for discontinuation
Continue fluoxetine indefinitely as a first episode of depression requires lifelong treatment
Stop fluoxetine and immediately start St John's Wort to prevent relapse
explanation: Fluoxetine (and its active metabolite norfluoxetine) has an exceptionally long half-life of 1–6 days (metabolite up to 16 days), which means it self-tapers naturally after cessation. This makes fluoxetine unique among SSRIs in that abrupt discontinuation rarely causes discontinuation syndrome. It is the only SSRI where immediate cessation is acceptable and in fact is sometimes deliberately used to manage discontinuation syndrome from other SSRIs. All other SSRIs (particularly paroxetine and venlafaxine) require gradual tapering.
wrongAnswerExplanations: This is the correct answer: fluoxetine's long half-life means it self-tapers and abrupt discontinuation does not cause the discontinuation syndrome seen with shorter-acting SSRIs.
Tapering over four weeks is the correct approach for shorter-acting antidepressants (paroxetine, venlafaxine, sertraline) but is not necessary for fluoxetine due to its long half-life.
Paroxetine has one of the SHORTEST half-lives among SSRIs and is most likely to cause discontinuation syndrome — switching to paroxetine before stopping would worsen the problem.

National guidelines generally recommend a minimum of 6–9 months of antidepressant treatment after full remission; lifelong treatment is not standard for a single uncomplicated episode without residual symptoms.
St John's Wort has significant drug interactions (it induces CYP enzymes) and serotonin syndrome risk if overlapped with SSRIs; this is not appropriate advice.
clinicalPearl: Fluoxetine's long half-life (1–6 days; metabolite 16 days) means it self-tapers on abrupt cessation — the only SSRI where immediate stopping is safe. All other SSRIs and SNRIs require gradual tapering to prevent discontinuation syndrome.
keyClue: Fluoxetine = longest half-life SSRI = no taper required. This is the opposite of paroxetine and venlafaxine which must be tapered slowly.
commonExamTrap: Applying the same tapering rules to all SSRIs without recognising fluoxetine's pharmacokinetic exception.
missedConcept: Half-life determines discontinuation syndrome risk; fluoxetine's unique pharmacokinetics make it the exception to the taper rule.
